title: Code Sandbox
subtitle: Write, run, and experiment with Builder pattern code in Java, C++, and Kotlin. Tests validate your implementation.

templates.label: Classic Builder
templates.description: Build a Meal object with a fluent Builder API. All fields set via chained setters, validation in build(). Run and watch the tests pass.

testCases.name: Complete meal builds correctly
testCases.description: Builder creates a fully-configured Meal with all fields
testCases.expectedOutput: [MEAL] Sesame bun, 2 patties, Cheddar cheese, BBQ sauce, Large -- $11.50

testCases.name: Minimal meal builds correctly
testCases.description: Builder creates a simple meal with basic options
testCases.expectedOutput: [MEAL] Plain bun, 1 patties, None cheese, Ketchup sauce, Small -- $5.99

testCases.name: Validation catches missing bun
testCases.description: build() throws when required field bun is missing

experiments.title: Add Spice Level
experiments.prompt: Add a spiceLevel field (MILD, MEDIUM, HOT) to Meal and the Builder. How many existing builder calls break?
experiments.hint: Zero calls break! That's the beauty of Builder -- all fields are optional setters. New field has a default. Existing code that doesn't call setSpiceLevel() keeps working. Compare this to adding a constructor parameter.

experiments.title: Add Validation
experiments.prompt: Add a constraint: if bun is 'Gluten-free bun', cheese cannot be 'Bacon cheese'. Where does this validation go?
experiments.hint: Put it in build(). The Builder accumulates state, and build() is the single validation checkpoint. Never validate in individual setters -- you'd need to check ordering. build() sees the complete picture.

experiments.title: Convert to Step Builder
experiments.prompt: Enforce ordering: setBun() MUST be called before setPatties(). Use interfaces to make the compiler enforce this.
experiments.hint: Create interfaces: BunStep { PattyStep setBun(String) }, PattyStep { CheeseStep setPatties(int) }, etc. Builder implements all. setBun() returns PattyStep, so you CAN'T call setPatties() first. Compiler enforces build order!

templates.label: Builder + Director
templates.description: Same Meal builder, but now a MealRecipes director creates pre-configured meals. Director knows HOW to build, Builder knows HOW to construct.

testCases.name: Kids meal via director
testCases.description: MealRecipes.kidsMeal() builds a small simple meal
testCases.expectedOutput: [KIDS] Small-regular bun, 1 patty, American cheese, Ketchup sauce, Small

testCases.name: Gourmet burger via director
testCases.expectedOutput: [GOURMET] Brioche bun, 2 patty, Gruyere cheese, Truffle aioli sauce, Large

testCases.name: Custom build bypasses director
testCases.description: Client can still use Builder directly without director
testCases.expectedOutput: [CUSTOM] Pretzel bun, 3 patty, Pepper jack cheese, Chipotle sauce, XL

experiments.title: Add Spice Level
experiments.prompt: Add a spiceLevel field to the Builder. How many director recipes need updating?
experiments.hint: Zero! Director recipes that don't call setSpiceLevel() get the default. Only add it to recipes that need it (e.g., spicyChicken()). The director isolates clients from builder changes.

experiments.title: Add Validation
experiments.prompt: Add a gluten-free + bacon constraint: if bun is 'Gluten-free bun', cheese cannot be 'Bacon cheese'. Where should validation live -- director or builder?
experiments.hint: In build()! The builder enforces invariants. The director is just a convenience layer for common recipes. If validation is in the director, someone bypassing the director could create an invalid Meal.

experiments.title: Convert to Step Builder
experiments.prompt: Convert to a Step Builder where setBun() must be called before setPatties(). Use interfaces to enforce ordering at compile time.
experiments.hint: interface BunStep { PattyStep setBun(String); }, interface PattyStep { CheeseStep setPatties(int); }, etc. Builder implements all interfaces. Each setter returns the NEXT step's interface. The director still works -- it just follows the step order.
